title: Daily Briefing — Wednesday, July 15
tldr: Web-design play of the day: you can now generate random values in pure CSS — no JavaScript — so scatter, jitter and stagger become one line each, but ship it as decoration that degrades. CSS has a new random() function: write rotate: random(-4deg, 4deg) and each element gets its own random tilt; animation-delay: random(0s, 1.2s) desynchronises a row of pulsing dots; random(--k, 0, 360) reuses the same roll across two properties on one element (a shared seed). This deletes the little JS loops we've all written to fake hand-placed variety — confetti, sticker walls, 'taped-on' photo grids, organic particle drifts. Honest scope: it shipped first in Safari 26.2 (unusual — WebKit led Chromium here), it is not Baseline (Chrome/Firefox not yet on stable), and an unsupported browser simply ignores the declaration — so it falls back to your base value with no breakage. Run it today: use it only for decorative properties (rotation, position, delay, hue), set a sensible base value first, guard anything load-bearing with @supports, and never randomise contrast-sensitive things like text colour. Card: card-webdesign-css-random.

Paid play: the ChatGPT ad channel just became measurable on money — OpenAI's Ads Manager added Attributed Sales Value, Sales ROAS and product-level reporting (Jul 14) — so wire up conversions + your feed and judge it on revenue, not clicks. Until now you could buy ChatGPT ads but only read impressions/clicks/CPC — you couldn't see the dollars back. Two new columns (Attributed Sales Value, Sales ROAS) plus per-product reporting change that: the answer-engine ad surface now speaks the same language as Google/Meta. Run it today in the OpenAI/ChatGPT Ads Manager: (1) create the campaign as a Product feed type (objective = Conversions) — budget type locks at creation, so choose daily-vs-total deliberately; (2) complete the 4-step conversion pipeline — add a data source, define conversion events (Purchase with value + currency), log those events via the API (it offers 'Use Codex' to generate the snippet), then link events to the campaign; (3) upload your product catalog via SFTP (the same title/price/availability feed you send Google Merchant Center); (4) read Sales ROAS at the product level and shift budget to the SKUs that actually return — but during launch trust your billing statement for spend, since dashboard spend lags. Card: card-paid-chatgpt-ads-measurement (+ the onboarding half, card-paid-chatgpt-ads-feed).

Social play: Instagram will now dub your Reel into another language with your own AI-cloned voice and lip-sync — from the composer, free — so your best video can earn a second, non-English audience with one toggle. On Jul 14 Meta expanded Reels AI translations to Japanese, Korean, French, German and Italian (on top of English/Spanish/Hindi/Portuguese and several Indian languages). It auto-dubs in a voice that mimics your tone and can lip-sync your mouth to the new audio, so it reads as if you filmed it in that language. Run it today: post a Reel that carries in any language (a demo, a tip, a story — not one riding a native-language pun), open the auto-translation option in the Reels composer (needs 1,000+ followers), pick up to two target languages, preview it, turn off lip-sync if it looks off, and abandon the translation entirely if it's wrong before publishing. Then watch reach + retention on the translated versions to see which markets respond. Honest scope: quality varies and sync can look imperfect — always review before it ships. Card: card-social-instagram-reel-translations.

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked; same-day market prints directional): the AI-capex bull case got a hard datapoint (ASML blew out and raised again) while the backlash got its first law (New York banned new data centers). ASML (the sole maker of the EUV machines every leading-edge AI chip needs) reported Q2 net sales of €9.3B (vs ~€8.8B est.) and a €2.9B profit, and — for the second time this year — raised its 2026 sales forecast, to €43–45B from €36–40B, guiding Q3 to €11–12B on AI-chip demand and adding EUV capacity; Intel will use its next-gen High-NA machines (~$400M each) for Panther Lake. Shares rose ~5%, SK Hynix jumped ~8% and Asia tech rallied. Against that, the physical bill is now political: New York became the first US state to halt new data-center construction (Hochul signed a ~1-year moratorium on new 50MW+ 'hyperscaler' sites Jul 14; NY residential power is up ~68% since 2019) — a Confirmed, 5-source story that hardens the 'power is the binding constraint' thread. Also cross-checked: Meta was sued by 26 current/ex-employees alleging its May layoffs used biased AI to target workers on protected leave/with disabilities (Confirmed, 5 sources); Apple opened its Gemini-rebuilt Siri to everyone in the iOS 27 public beta (Confirmed, 4). Predictions: none resolve today — the Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [due ~Jul 20] is now leaning held (ASML + SK Hynix strength), and the GLM-5.2 OpenRouter-top-3 [due ~Jul 19] and AAPL-reverses-WWDC [due ~Jul 21] calls are on the clock. No news card (landscape = intake; the durable craft payoff stays card-ai-tooling-model-portability).

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is the machine now does the last mile of work you used to hand-code, hand-buy, or hand-translate — so your job moves to setting the input and reading the result. CSS random() does the scatter you used to script — so you pick the property and the range, and let the engine roll. ChatGPT's Ads Manager finally shows Sales ROAS — so you stop guessing the channel's worth and let the revenue column rank your SKUs. Instagram dubs your Reel in your own voice — so you choose the video and the market and let AI open the door, then read which language answered. Even the landscape rhymes: ASML's blowout says the compute build-out is real, while New York's ban says someone has to pay the power bill — two readings of one input. Set the input well; judge on the output — never the pitch.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: generate random values in pure CSS with random(), no JavaScript — but ship it as decoration that degrades gracefully

blocks.sub: CSS's new random() function returns a per-element random value in a range you set — random(-4deg, 4deg) for tilt, random(0s, 1.2s) for a desynced animation-delay, random(--seed, 0, 360) to reuse one roll across two properties. It deletes the little JS loops we write to fake hand-placed variety (confetti, sticker walls, taped-photo grids, particle drifts). Shipped FIRST in Safari 26.2 (WebKit led Chromium), NOT Baseline (Chrome/Firefox not on stable yet), and unsupported browsers just ignore the declaration and fall back to your base value — so it's pure decorative progressive enhancement.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: for years, any 'organically scattered' UI — a wall of slightly-rotated stickers, confetti, a hand-placed photo collage, particles drifting at different speeds — meant a JavaScript loop that assigns each element a random inline style. CSS now has a native random() function (CSS Values & Units L4/5), and it does that job in the stylesheet. The grammar is random(MIN, MAX) or random(MIN, MAX, STEP), and any dimensional type works — <number>, <length>, <angle>, <time>, <percentage>, even <integer>. By default each element that matches the rule gets its own independent roll — that per-element caching is exactly what makes it useful for sibling variety with no :nth-child() ladder and no JS. You can also pass an optional sharing key: random(--size, 10px, 200px) means every property that uses --size on the same element draws the same underlying roll (so a badge's width and height match), while element-shared makes all elements share one value. Why it matters for us: this is the same 'delete-the-JS' win as the rest of the modern-CSS-primitives thread (sibling-index, scroll-triggered animations) — a decorative flourish that used to cost a script becomes one declaration, with no bundle, no hydration, no layout-thrash. Honest scope: random() shipped first in Safari 26.2 — an unusual inversion where WebKit led Chromium (Chrome and Firefox haven't shipped it to stable yet), so it is not Baseline. Because an unsupported browser treats rotate: random(-4deg, 4deg) as an invalid value and simply ignores that one declaration, it falls back to whatever base you set (no rotation) with zero breakage — the classic graceful-degrade primitive you can lead with. The real caveat is accessibility, not support: never let random() drive contrast-sensitive values (text colour, opacity on text) — a bad roll can produce unreadable low-contrast text.
blocks.doIt: (1) Use it only for decorative properties and set a sensible base first: .sticker { rotate: 0deg; rotate: random(-5deg, 5deg); } — the plain rotate: 0deg is your fallback, the random() line enhances it. (2) Desync a row of animations (pulsing dots, floating particles) with animation-delay: random(0s, 1.5s); animation-duration: random(2s, 3.5s); so no two tick in lockstep. (3) Scatter positions for a hand-placed collage: top: random(0%, 60%); left: random(0%, 80%); inside a positioned container. (4) Reuse one roll across related properties with a sharing key — width: random(--s, 40px, 120px); height: random(--s, 40px, 120px); keeps each item square while varying size element-to-element. (5) Guard anything load-bearing with @supports (rotate: random(0deg,1deg)) { … }, and keep clip-path/JS as your fallback only where the effect is essential rather than decorative. (6) a11y check: never randomise text colour, contrast, or anything that affects readability or focus order — decorative motion/rotation only, and respect prefers-reduced-motion. Card: card-webdesign-css-random.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (≥3) — MDN (primary reference: grammar, sharing keys `auto`/`element-shared`/`--dashed-ident`/`fixed`, supported types, explicit 'not Baseline' + the @supports-fallback pattern + the contrast a11y warning) plus CSS-Tricks (almanac entry + the 'What's !important #14' round-up that surfaced it) and SitePoint's production write-up; support state (Safari 26.2 first, Chrome/Firefox not-yet-stable) cross-checked against the search consensus / caniuse. Honest scope: WebKit-first and NOT Baseline → decorative progressive enhancement only; unsupported browsers fall back to the base value; keep random() away from contrast-sensitive properties. Advances thread-modern-css-primitives (the 'graceful-degrade primitive you can ship the day it hits one engine' branch — note the rare WebKit-first inversion). Logged a prediction (random() ships in a 2nd engine — Chrome or Firefox stable — by end of 2026, path to Baseline). Sources: MDN, CSS-Tricks, SitePoint.

sections.h: Paid — run it today: the ChatGPT ad channel is now measurable on revenue (Attributed Sales Value + Sales ROAS + product-level reporting), so wire up conversions and your feed and judge it on money, not clicks

blocks.sub: OpenAI's ChatGPT Ads Manager added Attributed Sales Value, Sales ROAS and product-level reporting (reported Jul 14 2026) — the answer-engine ad surface now reports revenue like Google/Meta, not just impressions/clicks/CPC. Run-it-today: create a Product-feed campaign (Conversions objective; budget type locks at creation), complete the 4-step conversion pipeline (data source → conversion events with value → log via API, 'Use Codex' generates the snippet → link events to the campaign), upload your catalog via SFTP (reuse your Google Merchant Center feed), then read Sales ROAS per product and reallocate to the SKUs that return. Trust your billing statement for spend during launch — dashboard spend lags.
blocks.p: What changed / the stakes: we already carded getting your products onto ChatGPT ads via a reused Shopping feed (card-paid-chatgpt-ads-feed). The gap was measurement — you could spend but only see impressions, clicks and CPC, which makes the channel impossible to defend to a client or a CFO. On Jul 14 2026 OpenAI's Ads Manager gained two revenue columns — Attributed Sales Value and Sales ROAS — plus product-level ad reporting, so the newest ad surface finally reports in the same currency (literally) as Google and Meta. Why it matters for us: buyers increasingly start product research inside ChatGPT, and this is a still-uncrowded surface — but 'uncrowded' is only worth chasing if you can prove return. Revenue + per-product reporting turns ChatGPT ads from a faith-based experiment into a channel you can optimise: put budget behind the SKUs whose Sales ROAS clears your target and cut the ones that don't. The setup mirrors Google Merchant Center closely, so most of the plumbing is familiar. Honest scope: this is a fast-moving beta — early reporting has spend-latency (spend accrues before it appears in the dashboard, so your billing statement is the authoritative spend source during launch), attribution windows/methodology aren't fully documented, and the tracking is server/API-based (a conversion-events pipeline, not a drop-in browser pixel today) — so budget a little engineering time and reconcile against your own analytics.
blocks.doIt: (1) Create the campaign as a Product-feed type in ChatGPT/OpenAI Ads Manager with the Conversions objective; choose your budget type (daily vs total) deliberately — it locks at creation and can't be changed later. (2) Build the 4-step conversion pipeline: add a data source → create conversion events (Purchase with value + currency, plus any lead/signup events) → log those events via the API (the UI offers 'Use Codex' to generate the integration code, and flags setup gaps) → link the events to your campaign. (3) Upload your catalog via SFTP — reuse the exact title/image/price/availability feed you already send Google Merchant Center; keep it synced so pricing/stock stay accurate. (4) Read Sales ROAS at the product level and reallocate: scale the SKUs that clear your ROAS target, pause the ones that don't, and benchmark against the same products' Google Shopping numbers. (5) Trust billing over the dashboard for spend during launch (reporting lags), and reconcile attributed sales against your own GA4/analytics before you fully believe the ROAS — treat early figures as directional. (6) Optional cross-check: tag destination URLs with UTMs (utm_source=chatgpt) so GA4 gives you an independent read on the conversions. Card: card-paid-chatgpt-ads-measurement (advances thread-ai-ad-surfaces; pairs with card-paid-chatgpt-ads-feed).
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Search Engine Roundtable first observed the new Attributed Sales Value + Sales ROAS + product-level columns (Jul 14), echoed by Optimixed; the setup mechanics (3-step campaign wizard, Standard/Product-feed types, 4-step conversion pipeline with 'Use Codex', SFTP catalog upload, customer-match, budget-type-locks-at-creation, spend-latency) are corroborated by Receptive Media's hands-on Ads-Manager first-look, and OpenAI's own product-feed help doc backs the feed side. Honest scope: fast-moving beta — attribution windows/methodology undocumented, server/API-based tracking (no simple browser pixel yet), dashboard spend lags billing → reconcile against first-party analytics, treat ROAS as directional at launch. Advances thread-ai-ad-surfaces (from onboarding → measurement). Bumped card-paid-chatgpt-ads-feed (added the new-metrics note). Logged a prediction (a 2nd answer-engine ad platform — Perplexity, Google AI Mode, or Microsoft Copilot — ships product-feed + revenue/ROAS reporting). Sources: Search Engine Roundtable, Optimixed, Receptive Media, OpenAI Help.

sections.h: Social — run it today: Instagram will dub your Reel into another language in your own AI voice, with lip-sync, free from the composer — so your best video can earn a second, non-English audience with one toggle

blocks.sub: Meta expanded Reels AI translations on Jul 14 2026 to Japanese, Korean, French, German and Italian (on top of English/Spanish/Hindi/Portuguese + several Indian languages). It auto-dubs your voice in a tone that mimics you and can lip-sync your mouth to the new audio, so the Reel reads as natively filmed. Enable it in the Reels composer (needs 1,000+ followers), pick up to two target languages, PREVIEW, switch off lip-sync if it looks off, or abandon the translation entirely before posting. Then watch reach + retention per language to see which markets respond. Quality/sync varies — always review before shipping.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: distribution on Reels has always been capped by language — a great English demo simply doesn't surface to a Japanese or German viewer. On Jul 14 2026 Meta widened its AI Reels translation feature to Japanese, Korean, French, German and Italian, adding to an existing set (English, Spanish, Hindi, Portuguese and several Indian languages) and a parallel Facebook rollout. The feature auto-dubs your spoken audio into the target language in a synthetic voice that mimics your tone, and optionally applies AI lip-sync so your mouth matches the translated words — the effect is a Reel that looks and sounds like you filmed it natively. Why it matters for us and our clients: this is a near-zero-effort reach lever. A single strong Reel — a product demo, a how-to, a founder story — can now be served to opted-in viewers in five major new markets without a re-shoot, a voice actor, or a third-party dubbing tool, and it plugs straight into the 'brief for the signal the platform ranks' discipline (a clear, on-topic Reel travels further, now across languages). The catch is quality control: AI dubbing and lip-sync are imperfect — tone can flatten, idioms and puns don't survive, and sync can drift — so this is a review-before-publish feature, not a fire-and-forget one. Honest scope: the auto-translation option lives in the Reels composer and requires 1,000+ followers to create (all public accounts can watch translated Reels); you select up to two languages per translation; and per Meta/Mosseri the exact count of supported languages is still settling (14–18 depending on the surface) — treat the specific list as current-but-moving.
blocks.doIt: (1) Pick a Reel that translates well: a demo, tutorial, tip or story that carries on substance — not a clip built on a native-language pun, rhyme or slang that won't survive dubbing. (2) Open the auto-translation option in the Reels composer (needs 1,000+ followers) and select up to two target languages from the expanded set (JA, KO, FR, DE, IT, ES, HI, PT, + Indian languages). (3) Preview every translation before posting — listen to the dubbed audio and, if the AI lip-sync looks off, turn syncing off (dub-only still works), or abandon the translation entirely if the meaning is wrong. (4) Keep on-screen text short and literal and put the key claim in the first 3 seconds so it survives translation and helps the model file your Reel under the right topic in each market. (5) Measure per language: track reach, watch-time/retention and follows on the translated versions to learn which markets actually respond, then make more for the ones that do. (6) Don't rely on it for compliance-sensitive or legal copy — have a native speaker check anything where a mistranslation carries risk. Card: card-social-instagram-reel-translations.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Social Media Today (composer location, 1,000+-follower requirement, two-languages-per-translation, review/switch-off-sync/abandon-before-posting, the 14-vs-18 language-count nuance), Meta's own creators.instagram.com translations post (primary: voice-mimicking dub + optional lip-sync, language list, all-public-accounts-can-watch), and WERSM (independent write-up of the language expansion). Honest scope: quality/sync imperfect (review before publishing); the exact supported-language count is still moving (14–18 by surface); Facebook has a 1,000+-follower gate and a partly different language set. Fits the 'organic social = design + measure for the signal the platform ranks' durable lesson (now across languages). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major short-video platform — TikTok, YouTube Shorts, or Snap — ships creator-facing AI voice-dubbing WITH lip-sync from its native composer). Sources: Social Media Today, Instagram (creators blog), WERSM.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked; same-day market prints directional): the capex bull case got a hard datapoint (ASML blew out and raised again) while the backlash got its first law (New York banned new data centers)

blocks.sub: Two cross-checked reads plus two confirmed policy/legal moves. (1) ASML — the sole EUV-lithography maker — posted Q2 net sales €9.3B (vs ~€8.8B est.) + €2.9B profit and RAISED 2026 guidance a 2nd time to €43–45B (from €36–40B), Q3 €11–12B, on AI-chip demand + more EUV capacity (Intel takes High-NA ~$400M machines for Panther Lake); shares ~+5%, SK Hynix ~+8%, Asia tech rallied. (2) New York became the FIRST US state to halt new data-center construction — Hochul signed a ~1-year moratorium on new 50MW+ hyperscaler sites (Jul 14; NY residential power +~68% since 2019). Also: Meta sued by 26 employees over allegedly biased-AI layoffs (Confirmed 5); Apple opened Gemini-rebuilt Siri to all in the iOS 27 public beta (Confirmed 4).
blocks.p: What moved (cross-checked; treat same-day market figures as directional). (1) The build-out is real — ASML blowout + a 2nd raise. ASML, which makes the EUV lithography machines every leading-edge AI chip depends on, reported Q2 net sales of €9.3B (above ~€8.8B est.) and a €2.9B net profit, and raised its full-year 2026 forecast for the second time this year — to €43–45B, from €36–40B (a ~16% midpoint lift), guiding Q3 to €11–12B (well above ~€10.1B consensus) and lifting gross-margin guidance to 54–56%. It's expanding EUV capacity, and Intel confirmed it will use ASML's next-gen High-NA machines (~$400M each) for its flagship Panther Lake laptop chips. Shares rose ~5%, SK Hynix jumped ~8%, and Asia tech rallied — a concrete, order-book datapoint that the AI-compute spend is still accelerating, not rolling over. (2) The bill comes due — New York's first-in-nation data-center moratorium. Gov. Kathy Hochul signed an order (Jul 14) pausing construction of new 'hyperscaler' data centers drawing 50MW+ for up to a year, halting state permitting while regulators write standards for energy, water and environmental impact — the first statewide data-center ban in the US, as NY residential electricity prices have climbed ~68% since 2019 and public opposition rises. The two stories are the same coin: ASML says the demand for AI compute is compounding; New York says someone has to supply — and pay for — the power, which hardens the standing 'power is the binding constraint' thread. Read for us: it's a climate signal, not a trade — lead client AI pitches with cost-per-outcome and a power/where-it-runs awareness, because energy and siting are now the gating variables. (3) Two more cross-checked landscape moves. Meta was sued by 26 current and former employees alleging its May layoffs used a biased AI system that disproportionately cut workers with disabilities or on protected leave (Confirmed, 5 sources) — a reminder that 'the AI made the decision' is becoming a legal-liability question wherever automated systems touch people. And Apple opened its Gemini-rebuilt Siri to everyone via the iOS 27 public beta (Confirmed, 4 sources) — the long-delayed Apple-Intelligence Siri is finally in real hands, advancing the Apple-as-AI-company thread from courtroom (this week's Apple-v-OpenAI suit) to shipping product. Predictions: none resolve today. The Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [due ~Jul 20] now leans held (ASML's raise + SK Hynix's ~8% pop + the Asia rally lift the basket); the Jun-19 GLM-5.2 OpenRouter-top-3 call [due ~Jul 19] and the Jun-9 AAPL-reverses-WWDC call [due ~Jul 21] are on the clock — judged at the window on closes, not today's tape. No news card (the landscape is intake; the durable craft payoff stays route-your-models / build-for-portability, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). Sources: CNBC, Techmeme/Reuters, EconoTimes, Investing.com (ASML + SK Hynix/Asia rally); Washington Post, CNBC, Fortune, Engadget, The Verge (NY data-center moratorium); The Verge, Ars Technica, CNBC, Engadget (Meta AI-layoff suit); The Verge, TechCrunch, Engadget (Siri iOS 27 public beta).

sources: MDN — random() CSS function (grammar, sharing keys, 'not Baseline', @supports fallback + a11y warning) · CSS-Tricks — random() almanac entry · CSS-Tricks — What's !important #14 (random(), field-sizing, gap decorations) · SitePoint — Native randomness in CSS without JavaScript (Safari 26.2, fallbacks) · Search Engine Roundtable — ChatGPT Ads Manager adds Attributed Sales Value & Sales ROAS + product reporting (Jul 14 2026) · Optimixed — ChatGPT Ads Manager adds revenue metrics + product reporting · Receptive Media — Inside the OpenAI/ChatGPT Ads Manager (campaign wizard, 4-step conversion pipeline, SFTP feed, budget locks) · OpenAI Help — Create campaigns from product feeds · Social Media Today — Instagram Reels adds more AI translations (Jul 14 2026) · Instagram for Creators — Meta AI translations for Reels (voice-mimicking dub + lip-sync) · WERSM — Meta expands AI translation for Reels to more languages · CNBC — ASML raises 2026 forecast a 2nd time on strong AI chip demand (Q2 €9.3B) · CNBC — SK Hynix jumps ~8% as Asia tech stocks rally · Washington Post — New York becomes first state to impose a data-center moratorium · CNBC — New York becomes first US state to impose an AI data-center ban · The Verge — Meta sued over allegedly biased-AI layoffs · The Verge — Apple's new Siri AI in the iOS 27 public beta (hands-on) · TechCrunch — Apple opens its new Siri AI to everyone with the iOS 27 public beta
